chunk_summary: This section outlines risk-tiered feeding strategies and follow-up schedules for preterm, LBW, and ELBW infants post-discharge, emphasizing structured nutrition plans and monitoring based on clinical risk factors and growth outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for assigning preterm, LBW, and ELBW infants to risk tiers for post-discharge nutrition.
Learn the default feeding strategies and follow-up cadence for each risk tier.
Identify key clinical conditions (e.g., EUGR, BPD, CHD) that necessitate higher-intensity nutrition plans.
Recognize the importance of early follow-up and structured nutrient enrichment in high-risk infants.
key_facts: Tier 3 (very high risk) includes infants born <32 weeks, birth weight <1.5 kg, or with conditions like EUGR, BPD, CHD, or post-NEC surgery.
Tier 2 (moderate risk) includes infants born 32–34 weeks, discharge weight 1.5–2.0 kg, or with mild respiratory morbidity or poor weight gain (<15 g/day).
Tier 1 (lower risk) includes late preterm infants (34–36+ weeks) with stable growth and no major comorbidities.
Follow-up cadence: Tier 3 within 24–72 hours, Tier 2 within 1 week, Tier 1 monthly.
Default feeding for Tier 3 includes nutrient-enriched human milk or post-discharge formula, while Tier 1 uses standard feeding without enrichment.
reference_quote: Nutrition for the preterm, VLBW infant after NICU discharge — https://www.aap.org/en/patient-care/newborn-and-infant-...
document_key_topics: Risk-tiered feeding strategies for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants
Follow-up schedules based on clinical risk
